summary: Gruber argues that Adobe's announcement of working on a Flash player for iPhone is essentially meaningless because Apple will never approve it. He points out that Flash would create an alternative app platform that bypasses the App Store, directly violating the iPhone SDK's prohibition on interpreted code. He notes that Apple has no competitive motivation to allow Flash since no rival phone with Flash is threatening iPhone sales. He advises Adobe to focus on Android instead, where Flash could actually create leverage against Apple.
key_points: A Flash player on iPhone would enable developers to bypass the App Store entirely, which Apple would never permit
The iPhone SDK explicitly prohibits downloaded interpreted code, making a Flash player a direct terms violation
Apple will only consider Flash if a competing phone with Flash starts taking iPhone sales — which Gruber predicts means never
Adobe should focus on building Flash for Android to create actual competitive leverage rather than publicly begging Apple
money_quotes: Guess what? Apple isn't going to publish it.

If there were a Flash player for the iPhone, you could write games and other software in Flash rather than in Cocoa Touch. And you could sell games and apps directly for the Flash player, completely circumventing the App Store. Does this sound like something Apple would allow?
If Apple doesn't want to allow a competing email client on the iPhone, why would they allow a competing (and cross-platform) application runtime environment?

Apple will not publish a Flash player for the iPhone unless and until there exists some other mobile phone that (a) does run Flash, and (b) starts taking sales away from the iPhone. Which, my guess is, means never.
If Adobe really wants to get Flash on the iPhone, they should shut up about the iPhone and start talking about and coding for Android.
An excellent implementation of Flash for Android would give Adobe some amount of actual leverage. Until then, Adobe's just embarrassing themselves every time they mention it.
key_insight: Adobe's public campaign for Flash on iPhone ignores the fundamental reality that Apple has zero incentive to allow a competing cross-platform runtime that would undermine App Store control.
